1:
The witnesses agree on the facts and part company chiefly over a matter of geography. The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene with care. Keil and Delitzsch observe that Naboth's vineyard lay hard by the palace, and that the title king of Samaria implies Jezreel — the present Zerin — was only a summer residence; Ahab wants the plot for a vegetable garden, in exchange or for money. Gill fixes the timing: this falls after the two Syrian campaigns in which Ahab, victorious, had let Benhadad, "a blasphemer, and injurious to him," go free.

The one real crux is where the vineyard stood. Ellicott lays it out honestly. The Septuagint omits which was in Jezreel and makes the ground lie near the threshing-floor of Ahab in Samaria; the "plot of ground" of 2 Kings 9:25–26 is clearly at Jezreel, yet the emphatic prediction of v.19 was fulfilled at the pool of Samaria (22:38), and the whole action touching Ahab seems to pass at Samaria. On the other side stand the plain reading of the text and the phrase his city in vv.8 and 11. Ellicott judges the Samaria location "on the whole the more probable," while confessing the discrepancy cannot be cleared up entirely with our present knowledge.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the moral above the map. Naboth's vineyard "unhappily lay near" the palace, and "many a man's possessions have been his snare, and his neighbourhood to greatness of pernicious consequence." To desire a convenience to one's estate is not evil — "the virtuous woman considers a field and buys it" — but to covet inordinately, even by lawful means, is "a fruit of selfishness," against the law of contentment and the letter of the tenth commandment, Thou shalt not covet thy neighbour's house. Henry adds the covenant background the others assume: Canaan was in a peculiar sense God's land, the Israelites his tenants, forbidden to alienate their lot save in extreme necessity, and then only till the jubilee (Lev. 25:28).

The irony that opens the tragedy is not lost on the witnesses. Henry observes that Ahab "had tamely quitted the great advantages God had given him of enlarging his dominion for the honour of his kingdom, by his victory over the Syrians, and now is eager to enlarge his garden, only for the convenience of his house" — as if, having been "pound foolish" in letting Benhadad go, he would be "penny wise" over a vegetable plot. Gill's note on the sparing of Benhadad and Keil's insistence that Jezreel was but a summer residence both serve the same picture: a king careless of great trusts yet consumed by a small greed. The vineyard is introduced, in the plain words of the verse, only that we may watch a covetous heart fasten on it; and the commentators agree that the whole horror to follow springs from a desire that, indulged, "exposes men to continual vexations."
2:
The voices are united on the surface courtesy of Ahab's request and on the deeper illegality of what he asks. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke grants that "the request of Ahab seems at first view fair and honorable" — a kitchen garden or grass-plot beside the palace, with a better vineyard or its full worth offered in exchange. Yet Clarke sees the trap: Ahab "wished him to alienate it finally," and this the law had expressly forbidden (Lev. 25:14–17; 25:25–28). To covet it "showed the depravity of Ahab's soul," and it is worth noting, Clarke says, that even these despotic kings "dared not seize on the inheritance of any man," for the Jewish kings "had no authority either to alter the old laws, or to make new ones."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch press the point of conscience: Naboth was "not merely at liberty as a personal right to refuse the king's proposal, but bound by the commandment of God," since the sale of a paternal inheritance was forbidden (Lev. 25:23–28; Num. 36:7). Ellicott finds the whole history "singularly true to nature." Ahab's opening offer is "courteous and liberal"; Naboth's refusal, grounded on the illegality and the natural dislike of alienating "the inheritance of his fathers," is "not only allowable, but right," even if it carries "a certain tone of harshness." What follows is pure character: Ahab "neither recognises the legality and justice of Naboth's action, nor dares to resent" it, but "like a spoilt child, he comes back sullen and angry."

Ellicott seals it with a comparison the reformed Gill would not dispute: all Ahab has "is as nothing, while the little plot of ground is refused," just as to Haman all was worthless while Mordecai sat in the king's gate (Esther 5:13). "This temper of sullen, childish discontent," Ellicott warns, "is the natural seedplot of crime, under the instigation of more determined wickedness" — a sentence that quietly forecasts Jezebel.

Clarke widens the lesson with a traveller's illustration. The Hindoos, he reports from Mr. Ward, "are as strongly attached to their homesteads as the Jews were," clinging "to the family inheritance with a fondness bordering on superstition" even when its head is far away and its buildings almost in ruins. The point is that Naboth's refusal is no eccentricity but the natural and lawful love of a man for the land God gave his fathers. All the witnesses meet at this judgment: the request wore the face of fairness, but its aim — a final alienation of covenant land — was one no faithful Israelite could grant and no righteous king should have asked. What Ellicott calls the "seedplot of crime" is thus already sown in v.2: a desire lawful in its object made lawless by its refusal to accept a righteous "no."
3:
With Naboth's refusal the chapter's moral hinge turns, and though only the reformed Gill speaks here at length, his reading opens the verse fully. Naboth answers, "The LORD forbid it me, that I should give the inheritance of my fathers unto thee." Gill explains that the inheritances of families "were not to be alienated to another family, or tribe, nor even to be sold, unless in extreme poverty, and then to return at the year of jubilee." Naboth was "a man in good circumstances, and under no necessity of selling"; and had he sold to the crown, the plot "becoming a part of the royal demesnes, would never revert to his family." So, "both out of regard to the law of God, and the good of his family," he would not part with it "at any rate."

The refusal, Gill insists, is not rudeness but conscience. It shows Naboth "a conscientious man," and marks him, in the common tradition, as one of the seven thousand "that would not bow his knee to Baal, and against whom Ahab had a grudge." The verse's own words carry the theology the other witnesses assume: the land is the LORD's, held in trust, and "the land shall not be sold for ever" (Lev. 25:23); no inheritance was to remove from tribe to tribe (Num. 36:7). Naboth's the inheritance of my fathers is thus an appeal to the whole covenant order, not a bargaining posture.

The pastoral point the passage lands on is the cost of such fidelity. Naboth would gladly have obliged his prince in any lawful thing, but "he must obey God rather than men, and therefore in this matter desires to be excused." That a faithful man's very faithfulness should shortly expose him to a judicial murder is the tragedy the following verses unfold; here, at least, the conscience is clear, the ground is God's, and the refusal is righteous.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry deepens the reading with a suggestion that lifts the refusal toward heaven: "Some conceive that Naboth looked upon his earthly inheritance as an earnest of his lot in the heavenly Canaan, and therefore would not part with the former, lest it should amount to a forfeiture of the latter." On that view the vineyard is more than acreage; it is a token of covenant standing, a pledge of the promised rest, which a believing man would not sell for any price a king could name. Henry adds that Ahab "knew the law, or should have known it, and therefore did ill to ask that which his subject could not grant without sin." So the witnesses leave the verse with the man of conscience standing where the whole chapter's weight will fall: he "would rather hazard the king's displeasure than offend God," and in that single sentence the martyr of Jezreel is drawn to the life.
4:
The witnesses read Ahab's sulk as the self-torment of a discontented heart, and they read it with striking unanimity. The reformed Gill notes that Ahab came home "heavy and displeased" in the very humour he had shown after the prophet's rebuke in the previous chapter (1 Kings 20:43). The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch sharpen the diagnosis: instead of respecting Naboth's tender scruple, Ahab went home "sullen and morose," lay down, turned his face to the wall (as in 2 Kings 20:2), and would eat no bread — "after the manner of sorrowful persons," in Seb. Schmidt's phrase, "who shrink from and refuse all conversation." It is a "childish mode of giving expression to his displeasure," and it exposes a man "sold under sin" (v.20) who "only wanted the requisite energy to display the wickedness of his heart in vigorous action."

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke turns the bed into a sermon. "Poor soul! he was lord over ten-twelfths of the land, and became miserable because he could not get a poor man's vineyard." The lesson he draws is universal: "That soul in which God dwells not, has no happiness; and he who has God has a satisfying portion." Every privation and cross "makes an unholy soul unhappy," and such a soul carries its own torment — "Where'er it goes is hell; itself is hell."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the moral into two maxims. Discontent "is a sin that is its own punishment, and makes men torment themselves"; and it "is a sin that is its own parent — it arises not from the condition, but from the mind." His proof is a portrait: "as we find Paul contented in a prison, so Ahab was discontented in a palace." With the wealth of a kingdom and the honours of a throne at command, "all avails him nothing without Naboth's vineyard"; and "those that are disposed to fret, however well off, may always find something or other to fret at."

Henry completes the portrait of the sulking king. Ahab "grew melancholy," threw himself on his bed, "would not eat, nor admit company," and "was perfectly sick for vexation," unable to "bear the disappointment." The commentators agree that such a temper is not merely pitiable but dangerous: "inordinate desires expose men to continual vexations," and a heart thus fretting is exactly the tinder that Jezebel's counsel will set alight. The verse, brief as it is, marks the moral pivot of the story. Ahab has met a lawful refusal; he might have submitted to God's law as Naboth did, or mastered his craving with "the thoughts of the mercies" he enjoyed. Instead he nurses the grievance in the dark, turned to the wall, and so leaves the door open for a "more determined wickedness" to enter and act for him. The sullenness of v.4 is the silence into which Jezebel will speak.
5:
The entrance of Jezebel darkens the whole story, and the witnesses treat her opening question as the first stroke of a settled malice. The reformed Gill takes it at face value: she "perceived he was low spirited," and asked the cause that she might "relieve him if possible." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch hear a harder tone already — the sequel, "Dost thou now exercise royal authority over Israel?" is read as "an ironical question," the emphatic thou carrying scorn: "I, if thou hast not courage enough to act, will procure thee the vineyard."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the verse its fullest treatment and its warning. "Nothing but mischief is to be expected when Jezebel enters into the story — that cursed woman" (2 Kings 9:34). "Under pretence of comforting her afflicted husband, she feeds his pride and passion, and blows the coals of his corruptions." Henry finds her question capable of two senses. In a good sense it might quiet him — "Does it become so great a prince to cast thyself down for so small a matter?... hast thou so rich a kingdom at command, and canst not thou be without this one vineyard?" We should indeed "quiet ourselves under our crosses with the thoughts of the mercies we enjoy, especially our hopes of the kingdom." But Jezebel means it in the bad sense: "Art thou a king? use thy prerogative, and take by force what thou canst not compass by fair means... give me but leave to make use of thy name, and I will soon give thee the vineyard."

From this Henry draws the abiding lesson that binds the schools together here: "Unhappy princes those are, and hurried apace towards their ruin, who have those about them that stir them up to acts of tyranny, and teach them how to abuse their power." The comfort Jezebel offers is the counsel that destroys; the marriage bond, meant for help, becomes the instrument of a husband's undoing.

Henry first grants Jezebel the form of a wife's duty before exposing the substance of her malice. "It became her to take notice of his grief, and to enquire into the cause of it," he says; "those have forgotten both the duty and affection of the conjugal relation that interest not themselves in each other's troubles." The tragedy is that a real office of love is turned to the service of murder: the concern that should have counselled contentment is bent to feed a guilty craving. The witnesses thus read the verse as a study in how the nearest bonds may become the deadliest snares. Gill's mild gloss — that she wished to "relieve him" — and Keil's ironical "Dost thou now exercise royal authority?" are not finally at odds; Jezebel does mean to relieve her husband, but by a relief that damns them both, procuring for his fretful desire the one thing conscience and covenant had denied it.
6:
Ahab's rehearsal of his grievance draws out one shrewd observation and one telling omission. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry catches the omission: Ahab tells Jezebel the story "yet invidiously concealed Naboth's reason for his refusal, representing it as peevish, when it was conscientious." Naboth had said, in effect, I may not — the LORD forbids it; Ahab reports it as I will not give it thee, a bare and stubborn denial. The half-truth is itself a small act of injustice, stripping a righteous refusal of its righteousness and leaving only an affront to be avenged.

The reformed Gill glosses the request plainly: Ahab had offered "to sell it him at his own price," so that the transaction wore every appearance of fairness. The tragedy is that a lawful-seeming offer met a lawful refusal, and the king could digest neither the loss nor the reason. His self-pitying summary — "he answered, I will not give thee my vineyard" — is the version Jezebel will act upon, and it is a version shorn of God's name.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch look ahead to what such a telling makes possible. Once Ahab's complaint is lodged, "the shameless woman" writes in his name and seals with the royal seal — the seal that, stamped on the writing rather than signed, gave a document "the character of a royal command," as still among Arabs, Turks, and Persians (compare Esther 8:13; Dan. 6:17). The concealment of Naboth's motive and the lending of the seal work together: the king keeps his conscience half-clear by not knowing all, while his authority does the very deed his sullenness desired.

The witnesses feel how much turns on this small dishonesty of report. By rendering Naboth's "I may not" as a mere "I will not," Ahab hands his wife a grievance stripped of its righteousness and ripe for revenge; he confesses the wound without confessing its cause, and so invites a remedy that no honest account of the matter could have justified. Keil traces what the telling sets in motion: Jezebel writes "in Ahab's name," seals the letters "with his seal," and sends them "to the elders and to the nobles that were in his city, dwelling with Naboth," the royal stamp lending a private malice the whole weight of the crown (Esther 8:13; Dan. 6:17). The reformed and grammatical-historical voices agree in the moral: a half-truth told to the wrong counsellor is the first act of the crime. Ahab keeps back God's name from the story, and the story that follows will be one from which God's name is wholly absent — until Elijah brings it back.
7:
Jezebel's taunt draws from the witnesses a shared verdict on the nature of her power and Ahab's weakness. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke states it most bluntly: "Naboth, not Ahab, is king. If he have authority to refuse, and thou have no power to take, he is the greater man of the two. This is the vital language of despotism and tyranny." The queen's contempt is that a king should be crossed at all.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott hears in her words a Shakespearean boldness of emphasis, "like the impatience of Lady Macbeth." First "the bitter irony of the question, Dost thou govern the kingdom of Israel, and yet suffer a subject to cross thy will?" — her "scornful wonder at one who lets I dare not wait upon I would." Then the half-contemptuous invitation, "eat bread, and let thine heart be merry," in which Ellicott traces "the same recognition of a self-indulgent weakness of nature" that Elijah had touched with "Get thee up, eat and drink" (1 Kings 18:41). Ahab, in her estimate, "is fit only to desire and to revel; it is for bolder spirits to act for good or for evil."

The reformed Gill fills in the argument behind the sneer: "Art thou not king of Israel? canst thou not do as thou pleasest?" — the tempter's flattery that a monarch's will is its own law, and that so "trifling a matter" as a vineyard should never be denied him. The three witnesses converge: Jezebel's creed is that the throne stands above the covenant, that royalty is licensed to seize what it cannot buy, and that a scruple of conscience in a subject is an insult to be crushed rather than a right to be honoured.

The witnesses set Jezebel's creed against the whole constitution of Israel. Where Naboth had appealed to the law that made the king a subject of God, she appeals to a will that makes the king a law to himself. Gill notes the Targum's paraphrase, that a monarch should simply "exert his kingly power and authority, and demand it"; Clarke names the doctrine for what it is, "the vital language of despotism and tyranny"; Ellicott hears the sneer of a spirit bolder and worse than her husband's. What unites the three readings is the recognition that this is the chapter's true collision — not between Ahab and Naboth over a plot of ground, but between two ideas of kingship. In the one, sovereignty stands under the covenant and dares not seize; in the other, sovereignty stands above it and takes what it will. Jezebel, "eat bread, and let thine heart be merry," is coaxing a weak king across that line, and the murder of Naboth is what lies on the far side of it.
8:
The forged letters expose Ahab's complicity precisely at the point where he tries to keep his hands clean. The reformed Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Clarke agree on the mechanics. Gill supposes Ahab "giving her leave, no doubt, to take his seal," though she did not "communicate her scheme to him, lest he should object to it." Clarke puts it more sharply: "She counterfeited his authority by his own consent; and he lent his signet to stamp that authority."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott dwells on the seal as the pledge of the crown. It bore "the name, or token, of the king, engraved on stone, and impressed on a lump of clay attached to the letter" (Job 38:14); the sealing was "the pledge of authenticity and authority" (Gen. 38:18; Neh. 9:38; Esther 3:10; Dan. 6:17). And because the seal was ordinarily carried on the person, its use "implies Ahab's knowledge that something is being done in his name, into which he takes care not to inquire." As to the address, Ellicott notes the letters went to Naboth's own city, "most naturally interpreted as Jezreel," though possibly Samaria; Jezebel "desires that neither Ahab nor she herself should appear in the matter," yet supplies the necessary authority in writing, "because without it the deed could not be done."

Here the witnesses draw a single moral, though they do not press it as a dispute: the guilt that Ahab hopes to shed by ignorance the seal fastens upon him. He will ask no questions (v.16), but his signet has already answered them. The willful not-knowing is not innocence; it is a king lending his name to murder while arranging not to hear of it.

The witnesses will not let Ahab hide behind the queen's initiative. Gill's supposition that she withheld her scheme "lest he should object" only sharpens the point Clarke and Ellicott press: the seal was Ahab's, carried on his person, and its use meant he knew that "something is being done in his name, into which he takes care not to inquire." That studied not-inquiring is itself a species of consent. Ellicott adds the shrewd observation that Jezebel "desires that neither Ahab nor she herself should appear in the matter," and yet must put the authority in writing, "because without it the deed could not be done" — the crown's own instrument is indispensable to the crime. The letters, sealed and sent to Naboth's own city, thus bind together the two who would each seem absent: the king who profits and the queen who plots. The willful ignorance the seal makes possible is the same ignorance Ahab will parade at the vineyard, and the same the prophet will refuse to honour.
9:
The order to "proclaim a fast" turns the machinery of religion into an instrument of murder, and the witnesses are chiefly occupied with that perversion. The reformed Gill supposes the fast pretended "some dreadful calamity coming upon the nation," so that fasting and humiliation were needed to avert it and to search out the guilty. He records Kimchi's alternative — that the word means "call an assembly or congregation," that is, "convene a court of judicature" — and adds a shrewd doubt: "if it was a public fast, why should it be proclaimed only in Jezreel, and not throughout the kingdom?"

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke reads the same intent: to "intimate that there is some great calamity coming upon the nation, because of some evil tolerated in it," while "set Naboth on high" means to "bring him to a public trial." The grammatical-historical Ellicott weighs both possibilities and the ambiguity of the command. The fast might be only "a cloak of religious observance," or, more probably, a way "to imply that some secret sin had been committed, which would draw down vengeance on the whole city," and so to prepare the false accusation. There is "a like ambiguity" in set Naboth on high — either "an exaltation of pretended honour, or the lifting up his head" for arraignment (Gen. 40:20). Ellicott notes the grim irony that "the whole scheme implies a return of the people to at least the outward observance of the Law of the Lord."

Beneath the detail the witnesses agree on the deepest scandal, which Matthew Henry names in his survey of the plot: "there is no wickedness so vile, so horrid, but religion has sometimes been made a cloak and cover for it." The fast is not a lapse from Jezebel's scheme but the very heart of it — piety counterfeited to lend the murder the sanction of heaven.

Henry lays bare the cunning of the pretence in detail. The city is to be made "apprehensive of some dreadful judgment," and told "to be afraid that there is some great offender among them undiscovered, for whose sake God is angry"; the people are charged to inform against him, "and at last let Naboth be fastened upon as the suspected person, probably because he does not join with his neighbours in their worship." The fast, in other words, is not incidental to the frame but its very engine: it manufactures a public dread that only a public victim can appease, and it marks the one man of tender conscience as the likely cause of the city's peril. The witnesses converge on the bitter irony Ellicott names — that "the whole scheme implies a return of the people to at least the outward observance of the Law" — for the murder can only be dressed as justice if the city first puts on the garb of penitence it does not feel.
10:
The suborned witnesses and the charge they must swear draw the commentators into close philological work. The reformed Gill glosses "sons of Belial" as "worthless wretches, that have cast off the yoke of the law," lawless men who "will swear anything," set "right against Naboth to confront him, and accuse him to his face." The wesleyan-arminian Clarke supplies the legal ground: two were required because "life could not be attainted but on the evidence of two witnesses at least" (Deut. 17:6). The accusation, "Thou didst blaspheme God and the king," means "Thou art an atheist and a rebel."

Here a genuine debate opens over the verb. Clarke notes that the word is barach, which literally means "to bless," and that Parkhurst would render the charge, "Thou hast blessed the false gods and Molech" — an idolatry punishable by death (Deut. 13:6; 17:2). Clarke himself judges "the first meaning" — a euphemism for cursing or blasphemy — "the most simple," and protests against the notion that one word should yield "blessing and cursing," calling such opposite senses "very dangerous." The grammatical-historical Ellicott takes the softer line: the word "properly signifies to bless; thence, to part from with blessing; finally, to part from, or disown," so that the charge is "rather to renounce than to blaspheme" — though "the punishment, however, was stoning, as for positive blasphemy" (Lev. 24:16).

Ellicott anchors the two witnesses in the law that required them (Num. 35:30; Deut. 17:6), the very statute Jezebel exploits. The dreadful symmetry is plain to all the witnesses: the forms God gave to protect innocent life — two witnesses, a solemn charge, a lawful court — are turned, letter by letter, into the apparatus of judicial murder.

Henry draws out how neatly the double charge fits Ahab's purpose. To accuse Naboth of "blaspheming God and the king" is "a complicated blasphemy," and the pairing is not careless: "his blaspheming God would be the forfeiture of his life, but not of his estate, and therefore he is also charged with treason, in blaspheming the king, for which his estate was to be confiscated, that so Ahab might have his vineyard." The very shape of the indictment is bent to the coveted end. The commentators thus read the two false witnesses and the two-headed charge as one machine, every part of it borrowed from the law of God — the requirement of two mouths, the capital penalty for blasphemy, the forfeiture of a traitor's goods — and every part turned against the innocent. Whether the fatal word is best rendered "curse," "blaspheme," or "renounce," all agree with Ellicott that "the punishment was stoning, as for positive blasphemy" (Lev. 24:16), and that the whole proceeding is lawlessness clothed in the letter of the Law.
11:
The obedience of the magistrates astonishes the witnesses more than the malice of the queen. The reformed Gill grants that Jezebel might contrive such a scheme, and that "sons of Belial among the common people" might swear to falsehoods; "but that the elders and nobles of the city, the chief magistrates thereof, should be so sadly and universally depraved as to execute such a piece of villany, is really surprising." His verdict is a single sentence with a long reach: "Idolatry, when it prevails, takes away all sense of humanity and justice."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads their compliance as the signature of a particular political disease. "The pains taken in the invention of this foul plot, and the ready acquiescence of the rulers," are "characteristic of the baser forms of organised Eastern despotism — not venturing to take life by simple violence without some cause apparently shown, and yet always able to poison the springs of justice, and do murder under form of law." And he adds what makes the crime worse in Israel than elsewhere: here "the king was held to be but a vicegerent of God, subject, in theory, under the old constitution or manner of the kingdom (1 Samuel 10:25), to the supreme law," so that "the need of clothing crime with legal form would be especially felt."

Keil and Delitzsch, surveying the same obedience, call it "a striking proof both of deep moral corruption and of slavish fear of the tyranny of the ruthless queen." Between them the witnesses name the two forces that bend a whole magistracy to murder: a religion so corrupted that conscience is silenced, and a fear so complete that no elder dares to be the one who refuses. The tragedy is not that Jezebel commanded, but that a city obeyed.

Henry marks the smoothness of the obedience as its own indictment: the rulers "did as it was written in the letters, neither made any difficulty of it, nor met with any difficulty in it, but cleverly carried on the villany," whether "because they feared Jezebel's cruelty or because they hated Naboth's piety, or both." Between the fear and the hatred the whole magistracy is bent. The witnesses feel the horror of a corporate crime in which no single hand is stayed by conscience, and they read it as the fruit of the idolatry the chapter keeps naming: where the true God is forsaken, "all sense of humanity and justice" goes with Him. Keil's "slavish fear," Gill's "universally depraved," and Ellicott's "poison the springs of justice" are three names for one collapse — a city that keeps the outward forms of law while emptying them of righteousness, and so becomes, in the place of judgment, the very seat of wickedness.
12:
This brief verse records the execution of the plot, and the witnesses read its very bareness as a comment on the corruption behind it. The reformed Gill glosses "they proclaimed a fast" as "called a court" — the solemn assembly convened, as before, less to humble the city than to condemn a man. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that "the elders of Jezreel executed this command without delay," and repeat their judgment that it is "a striking proof both of deep moral corruption and of slavish fear of the tyranny of the ruthless queen."

What the verse withholds is as telling as what it states. There is no hesitation recorded, no objection lodged, no elder who stands apart; the letters are simply obeyed. The forms of piety and of justice — a fast, a court, a man "set on high" among the people — are all present, and all counterfeit. The very orderliness of the proceeding is its horror: a city keeps the outward shape of the Law of the LORD while emptying it of every righteous purpose, so that the more religious the ceremony appears, the more complete the guilt beneath it.

The witnesses thus let the verse do quietly what the earlier ones argued openly: they show, rather than say, that "there is no wickedness so vile but religion has sometimes been made a cover for it," in Henry's phrase. The stage is now set with false solemnity, the two false witnesses are about to take their places, and the reader understands that the machinery grinding toward Naboth's death is one that God had given for Naboth's protection. The next verse only completes what this one has, with dreadful economy, already made inevitable.

Henry names the deepest scandal the verse enacts without a word of protest: that the forms of religion themselves are pressed into the service of murder. "There is no wickedness so vile, so horrid, but religion has sometimes been made a cloak and cover for it," he writes; and he adds the caution that we "must not think at all the worse of fasting and praying for their having been sometimes thus abused, but much the worse of those wicked designs that have at any time been carried on under the shelter of them." The witnesses thus refuse two errors at once — despising the ordinance because hypocrites have profaned it, and excusing the hypocrisy because it wore the ordinance. The fast of Jezreel is a real solemnity emptied of its soul, a court convened to condemn the righteous, a whole city "set on high" in false devotion around one man marked for death. Its very orderliness, the commentators insist, is the measure of its guilt.
13:
The false trial reaches its verdict, and the witnesses attend to its procedure, its scope, and its cover of legality. The reformed Gill notes that the two men "sat before him," a position that "showed that they were his accusers," for it was the custom, as later among the Romans, "for the accusers and accused to be set face to face." He records the textual point that Josephus counts three witnesses, "but the Targum, and all the ancient versions, have only two" — the precise number the law demanded, now turned against the innocent.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke draws out the wider ruin. Because they charged him with treason "against God and the king," it is likely "they destroyed the whole of his family; and then the king seized on his grounds as confiscated." He points to the later oracle as proof: "the blood of Naboth, and the blood of his sons" (2 Kings 9:26). The grammatical-historical Ellicott adds the sombre detail that they "carried him forth," as was usual, "in order to avoid polluting the city with blood" — "possibly to his own ground, the coveted vineyard itself."

Beneath the machinery the witnesses feel the enormity that Matthew Henry names: the adulterous power that would not be content with the land but "must hunt for the precious life" (Prov. 6:26). Every safeguard is present and every safeguard is perverted — the two witnesses, the public assembly, the sentence carried out beyond the walls — so that murder wears the whole dress of justice. That the deed is done on Naboth's own inheritance, the very plot Ahab coveted, gives the crime its bitter completeness and prepares the ground, quite literally, for the word of the LORD that will meet Ahab there.

Henry follows the crime past Naboth to his household. The magistrates "punctually observed all the particulars" of the orders, and, "as it should seem, his sons with him, or after him," were slain — for when God "came to make inquisition for blood" the reckoning names "the blood of Naboth and the blood of his sons" (2 Kings 9:26). The children, Henry suspects, were "secretly murdered, that they might not claim their father's estate nor complain of the wrong done him," so that the confiscation might stand unchallenged. The witnesses feel the completeness of the injustice: not one man only but a family is destroyed under the color of law, the innocent stoned beyond the walls while the guilty prepare to take possession. And they hear, beneath the smooth procedure, the cry of the blood that will not be silenced — the "inquisition" of a God who keeps silence for a season but never forgets, and who will meet the beneficiary of this murder in the very field it was designed to secure.
14:
The report of the murder is chilling in its brevity, and the witnesses let that brevity speak. The reformed Gill marks the grim completeness of the message: Naboth was "not only stoned, but stoned to death." He explains why the notice went to the queen rather than the king: "though the letters came in Ahab's name, they might be privately informed that this was a scheme of Jezebel's; besides, they knew she had the sway at court, and especially that the news of this man's death would be acceptable to her" — the more so "if he was of the seven thousand that would not bow the knee to Baal."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the whole plot, notes how the elders "sent notice to Jezebel very unconcernedly," as though delivering "a piece of agreeable news." That is the verse's true horror: a judicial murder reported as an errand successfully run, evil announced with the ease of good tidings. Henry's reflection reaches for the wider scandal of "wickedness in the place of judgment" (Eccl. 3:16), and the tears of "the oppressed that have no comforter, while on the side of the oppressors there is power" (Eccl. 4:1).

Behind the flat sentence the witnesses hear the silence of heaven — a silence that Henry insists is only for a time: "hitherto God kept silence, did not intercept Jezebel's letters, nor stay the process of the elders." The blood of Naboth has now been shed, and though the report treats it as a matter closed, the reader is meant to feel, with the commentators, that it is a matter opened before God. The businesslike words is stoned, and is dead are the last the conspirators will speak in triumph; the next voice in the story will be the LORD's.

Henry draws a warning from the elders' ready compliance that reaches beyond this day. As obsequious as the rulers of Jezreel were to Jezebel's murderous orders, so obsequious would the elders of Samaria later prove to Jehu's, when he sent for the heads of Ahab's seventy sons (2 Kings 10:6–7). "Those tyrants that by their wicked orders debauch the consciences of their inferior magistrates may perhaps find at last the wheel return upon them," for "those who will not stick to do one cruel thing for them will be as ready to do another cruel thing against them." The witnesses thus read the flat report of v.14 not as the story's end but as the turning of a slow wheel of providence. The blood so casually reported is entered in a heavenly account; the elders who kill on command will one day kill their masters' house on command; and the silence of God, which the conspirators mistake for absence, is only the pause before the LORD makes His own inquisition for the innocent.
15:
Jezebel's word of triumph and Ahab's swift descent to the vineyard bring the witnesses to the question of the confiscated inheritance and the meeting that awaits. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain the legal ground: because "Naboth's sons were put to death at the same time" (2 Kings 9:26), the king could confiscate the property — "not on any rule laid down in the Mosaic law, but according to a principle involved in the very idea of high treason," so that "as, in the case of blasphemy, the property of the criminal was forfeited to the Lord as cherem (Deut. 13:16), the property of traitors was regarded as forfeited to the king." Ellicott says the same: with the heirs murdered "there was none to claim the inheritance," and in any case "the property of executed traitors would naturally fall to the king, although no enactment to this effect is found in the Law."

Keil also looks ahead to the encounter this possession provokes: Elijah's "Hast thou murdered and also taken possession?" was framed "to sharpen his conscience, since Ahab was obliged to admit the fact." The wesleyan-arminian Clarke, characteristically cautious, adds only that "by what rites or in what forms" the taking of possession was done "we do not know."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry supplies the pathos and the theology. Ahab is "so pleased with this accession to his estate" that he goes down in state to claim it himself — Jehu long remembered the day (2 Kings 9:25). Whatever the legal fiction — the estate claimed "in default of heirs" or "as forfeited by his crime" — Henry sees only naked power: "Might often prevails against right, and wonderful is the divine patience that suffers it to do so." God "is of purer eyes than to behold iniquity, and yet for a time keeps silence when the wicked devours the man that is more righteous than he" (Hab. 1:13).

Henry weighs the several titles by which Ahab might claim the ground and finds them all hollow. If Naboth's sons were dead, "he thought himself entitled to the estate in default of heirs"; if not, then "Naboth dying as a criminal, he claimed it as forfeited by his crime"; and "if neither would make him a good title, the absolute power of Jezebel would give it to him, and who would dare to oppose her will?" Behind every pretext stands the naked fact of might. The witnesses let this become the chapter's darkest moment before the dawn: innocent blood shed, an inheritance seized, a king rejoicing in his plunder, and heaven still silent. Yet the silence, Henry insists, is the "wonderful divine patience" of a God "of purer eyes than to behold iniquity" (Hab. 1:13), who suffers the wicked to devour the righteous only for a time. Jezebel says "Arise, take possession"; the reader knows another word is on its way to that same vineyard.
16:
Ahab's eager journey to seize the vineyard becomes for the witnesses a study in willful ignorance, and the interest gathers on a single manuscript question. The reformed Gill records simply that Ahab, informed by Jezebel of Naboth's death, "rose up to go down" and take possession. The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the king's character in his very silence: "It is characteristic of Ahab that he takes care to ask no question about Naboth's death, desirous to be innocent of the knowledge, and yet tacitly to applaud the deed. The guilt is Jezebel's; the fruit, his own."

Ellicott then weighs a curious addition in the Septuagint, which inserts here "he rent his clothes and put on sackcloth," representing Ahab "as struck with momentary horror, and then, after thus salving his conscience, still resolving to carry out his desire for the coveted vineyard." The picture, he grants, "is equally true to nature, especially to such a nature as his" — yet "the insertion has little authority, and is probably a mistaken interpolation" borrowed from the true repentance of v.27. The witnesses thus decline to soften the verse: whatever momentary qualm the Greek supplies, the Hebrew shows a man going down to enjoy a stolen field with no recorded scruple at all.

The moral all the commentators feel, though they leave it mostly unspoken here, is the same one the seal raised in v.8: a guilt hoped to be shed by not-knowing is not thereby shed. Ahab arranges to be ignorant of the murder and hastens to profit by it, and it is exactly this posture — innocent of the knowledge, greedy for the fruit — that Elijah will shatter in the next verses by collapsing the distance between the two: "Hast thou killed, and also taken possession?"

The witnesses press the point that ignorance sought is not innocence gained. Ahab "takes care to ask no question," and hastens down to enjoy the field — the posture of a man who wants the fruit of a crime while disowning its knowledge. But the seal he lent in v.8 has already answered the questions he declines to ask, and the possession he now takes will, in Henry's phrase, make him "guilty ex post facto." Ellicott's judgment on the Septuagint's inserted sackcloth only underlines the Hebrew's severity: whatever momentary horror a later scribe wished to lend the king, the true text shows no scruple at all between the murder and the seizure. The commentators thus set the stage precisely. Ahab goes down innocent in his own eyes and guilty in God's, and the whole self-deception is about to be dissolved by a single question that joins the two acts he has labored to keep apart — "Hast thou killed, and also taken possession?"
17:
Heaven breaks its silence, and the witnesses mark both the man sent and the moment chosen. The reformed Gill confesses that where Elijah had been, or how employed, "is not certain," but conjectures that during the Syrian wars the prophet "spent his time in founding or reviving the schools of the prophets." The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the reappearance theologically: "we have heard nothing of him since the call of Elisha, as though he had once more retired to solitude." In "the mere political service of the preceding chapter," important to the world, Elijah "takes no part; but emerges now for the higher moral duty of rebuking crime, and avenging innocent blood, in what Eastern tyranny would deem a very trivial matter."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry presses the fitness of the sending. A prophet "of lower rank" had once brought Ahab a message of kindness (1 Kings 20:13); now "the father of the prophets is sent to try him, and condemn him, for his murder." The place is Naboth's vineyard and the time the very act of taking possession: "then, and there, must his doom be read him." By taking possession Ahab "avowed all that was done, and made himself guilty ex post facto." Henry hears the confronting question behind Elijah's errand — "What hast thou to do in this vineyard, purchased with blood (Hab. 2:12), when thou hast caused the owner thereof to lose his life?" (Job 31:39).

The witnesses do not divide; they agree that the long patience of God has an end, and that the prophetic word, when it comes, comes precisely where the sin was consummated. Elijah, silent through the world's wars, is roused for the murder of one poor man — a measure, the commentators imply, of how differently heaven weighs innocent blood than kings and courts do.

Henry underlines that the long forbearance of God had a purpose and now has an end. "Hitherto God kept silence, did not intercept Jezebel's letters, nor stay the process of the elders of Jezreel; but now Ahab is reproved, and his sin set in order before his eyes." The commentators find a fitness in every circumstance of the sending: the messenger is not a lesser prophet but "the father of the prophets"; the place is the stolen vineyard; the time is the very hour of possession. Gill's conjecture that Elijah had spent the interval "founding or reviving the schools of the prophets" and Ellicott's picture of a prophet emerging from solitude "for the higher moral duty of rebuking crime, and avenging innocent blood," both make the same point — that this errand, in the world's eyes a trifle, is in heaven's eyes the weightiest business of the reign. The God who took no part in the Syrian wars rouses His greatest prophet for one murdered vinedresser.
18:
The commission to meet Ahab carries a small geographical phrase that the witnesses find surprisingly weighty. The reformed Gill reads "which is in Samaria" of the king's "seat there, his palace where he dwelt," noting that some would rather connect the clause with Israel, making it part of Ahab's title as king over the tribes. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke adds the chronology, drawn from the later narrative: this was "the next day after the murder" (2 Kings 9:26).

The grammatical-historical Ellicott refuses to let the phrase be smoothed away. "These words are almost unmeaning," he insists, "unless they literally signify that Ahab was then in Samaria, not in Jezreel. To interpret them as simply part of Ahab's title, or as signifying the country, not the town of Samaria, is to explain them away." The clause thus becomes a small but stubborn witness in the larger question raised at the chapter's opening — where the vineyard lay and where the crime was done — and it tells, in Ellicott's judgment, for Samaria.

The point is not antiquarian merely. If Ahab was in Samaria, then the prophet's word that dogs should lick the king's blood "in the place where dogs licked the blood of Naboth" bears on the pool of Samaria where that blood was at last washed away (1 Kings 22:38). The witnesses, reading a single half-verse of direction, are already tracing the line that runs from the coveted ground to the appointed place of judgment; the LORD who sends Elijah down knows exactly where Ahab stands, and exactly where he will fall.

The witnesses treat this half-verse of direction as a small hinge on which the chapter's geography turns. Gill's palace "at Samaria," Clarke's dating of the meeting to "the next day after the murder," and Ellicott's insistence that "which is in Samaria" must mean Ahab was literally there — all converge to locate the king, at the moment of judgment, in the capital rather than at Jezreel. Ellicott's refusal to "explain them away" is of a piece with his reading of v.1: the text is quietly consistent about where Ahab stands, and it stands where the dogs will one day lick his blood, at the pool of Samaria (1 Kings 22:38). What the commentators draw from so slight a phrase is the exactness of the divine knowledge. The LORD who sends Elijah "down to meet Ahab" needs no report of the king's movements; He knows the place of the crime, the place of the man, and the place of the coming judgment, and He sends His prophet to the precise spot where guilt and sentence will meet.
19:
Elijah's twofold word — the accusation and the sentence — is where the witnesses openly divide, though only over the manner of its fulfilment. The reformed Gill unfolds the charge: the murder is "ascribed to him, because his covetousness was the cause of it," and though "it was done by the contrivance of his wife," Ahab "by taking possession upon it, he abetted the fact." The accusation, "Hast thou killed, and also taken possession?", fastens the whole crime on the king who hoped to keep his hands clean.

Over the sentence — "In the place where dogs licked the blood of Naboth shall dogs lick thy blood, even thine" — the commentators part. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke denies a literal fulfilment on Ahab in person: his humbling deferred the stroke to his son (v.29), so that "dogs did lick the blood of Ahab, but it was at the pool of Samaria" (22:38), while it was Jehoram's body that was cast into "the portion of the field of Naboth" (2 Kings 9:25). Clarke even hears a pagan echo of the same fitness in Sophocles, where Orestes bids Aegisthus, "Go where thou slew'st my father, that in the self-same place thou too may'st die." The grammatical-historical Ellicott resists this transfer: "the marked particularity and emphasis of that sentence — even thine — preclude all explanations which would seek its fulfilment in the fate of Jehoram; nor can such explanations be justified by reference to v.29, for it is not this part of the sentence which is deferred by Ahab's repentance."

The division is real but bounded. All the witnesses hold that the word was fulfilled to the letter; they differ only over where and in whom — the pool of Samaria and the son, or the very ground at Jezreel and Ahab himself. Beneath the dispute lies a shared conviction: that divine justice answers the crime in its own coin and, often, in its very place.

The reformed Gill notes the small textual variant that does not soften the charge: the Vulgate and Arabic read the words "without an interrogation" — "thou hast killed, and hast taken possession" — turning the question into a bare indictment, but the sense is the same either way. What the witnesses will not surrender is the twin conviction that binds their debate together: that the covetousness which drove the murder is laid squarely on Ahab, and that the sentence, however fulfilled, was fulfilled to the letter. Clarke traces the letter to the pool of Samaria and to Jehoram's body in Naboth's field; Ellicott holds the emphatic "even thine" to the king's own person and the very ground of the crime. Between them stands a shared reverence for a justice so exact that it answers blood with blood in the appointed place — the "self-same place," in the pagan poet's phrase Clarke cannot resist quoting, where the slayer is himself slain. The dispute is over the map; the doctrine is one.
20:
The meeting of king and prophet is read by the witnesses as a study in the terror of a guilty conscience. The reformed Gill notes that Ahab greets Elijah as "mine enemy" because the prophet "dealt faithfully with him, and reproved him for his sins." Matthew Henry, surveying the scene, sees both hatred and fear: the last time they parted they parted friends (1 Kings 18:46), but now Ahab "had relapsed, and was worse than ever," and "his conscience told him he had made God his enemy." The sight of Elijah "was like that of the handwriting upon the wall to Belshazzar" (Daniel 5:6), so that a man's condition "is very miserable that has made the word of God his enemy" (Gal. 4:16).

The grammatical-historical Ellicott hears in the cry "partly of dismay, partly of excuse." Ahab, "having no word of defence to utter, endeavours to attribute Elijah's rebuke and condemnation to simple enmity," much as he had once cried, "Art thou he that troubleth Israel?" (1 Kings 18:17). "The crushing answer is that the prophet came not because he was an enemy, but because Ahab had sold himself — had become a slave instead of a king — under the lust of desire and the temptation of Jezebel."

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke fixes on that phrase, "thou hast sold thyself to work evil": "Thou hast totally abandoned thyself to the service of sin. Satan is become thy absolute master, and thou his undivided slave" (compare Rom. 7:14). The witnesses converge without dispute: Ahab miscalls the prophet his enemy, but the enmity is his own — a man at war with the word that would have saved him, and enslaved to the very desire that has now destroyed him.

Henry lingers on the two passions that struggle in Ahab's greeting. That he hates Elijah is plain — "the last time we found them together they parted very good friends" (1 Kings 18:46), but Ahab "had relapsed, and was worse than ever," and "could not endure him that would have helped him to recover himself." That he fears him is plainer still: "Hast thou found me? intimating that he shunned him all he could, and it was now a terror to him to see him." The witnesses read the whole exchange as the collision of a hardened will with the word that would have saved it. Ellicott's Ahab hides behind the charge of "enmity"; Clarke's Ahab has become "Satan's undivided slave"; Henry's Ahab trembles like Belshazzar at the writing on the wall. And all three land on the same reversal: the prophet whom the king brands his enemy is the one true friend left to him, and the enmity Ahab dreads is the enmity he has himself contracted against God.
21:
The sentence upon Ahab's house draws the witnesses to the weight of a dynastic judgment. The reformed Gill takes the opening threat, "Behold, I will bring evil upon thee," of Ahab's own person first — "cutting him off with a violent death, though his family was so numerous, having no less than seventy sons." The judgment falls on the head before it falls on the house.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the full extent of the doom. The LORD will "take away thy posterity, and cut off from Ahab every male, him that is shut up and left in Israel," and "make thine house like the house of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, and like the house of Baasha the son of Ahijah." The ground of the sentence is named twice over: "the provocation wherewith thou hast provoked me to anger, and made Israel to sin." It is not the murder alone but the whole apostasy of a reign that brings the axe to the root.

The witnesses feel the terror of a judgment that reaches beyond the sinner to his line. Naboth had guarded "the inheritance of his fathers" and lost his life; Ahab, who seized that inheritance, is told his own inheritance — his sons, his name, his throne — shall be swept away. The very sin of grasping after another's posterity of land recoils upon the grasper's posterity of blood. And the naming of Jeroboam and Baasha is deliberate: Ahab is set in the line of the kings whose houses God had already destroyed, warned that he has bought their doom with their idolatry, and that the same God who spared not the founding dynasties of the northern kingdom will not spare his.

The witnesses feel the exact justice in the shape of the doom. Naboth had died to keep "the inheritance of his fathers" for his sons; Ahab, who seized that inheritance over the bodies of Naboth's sons, is now told that his own sons — all seventy — shall be cut off, "him that is shut up and left in Israel." The grasp after another man's posterity of land recoils upon the grasper's posterity of blood. Keil's careful listing of the sentence and Gill's note that the stroke falls first on Ahab's own person combine to show a judgment as total as the crime was cold: the head cut down by a violent death, the house rooted out to the last male. And the naming of Jeroboam and Baasha is no rhetorical flourish; it fixes Ahab's place in a known succession of ruined dynasties, warning him that the God who kept His word against them will keep it against him, and that neither the number of his sons nor the strength of his throne will buy a single day's exemption from a sentence he has "provoked."
22:
The likening of Ahab's house to those of Jeroboam and Baasha is brief in the text, and only the reformed Gill comments directly, but his note opens the verse's whole force: these were "two of his predecessors, whose families were utterly destroyed." To be told that one's dynasty shall be "like the house of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, and like the house of Baasha the son of Ahijah" is to be placed in a line already known for its total extirpation.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the reason the sentence itself attaches: it is "for the provocation wherewith thou hast provoked me to anger, and made Israel to sin." That double charge — provoking God and corrupting the nation — is the standing indictment of the northern kings, and it had already brought the dogs and the fowls upon Jeroboam's line (1 Kings 14:11) and upon Baasha's (1 Kings 16:4). Ahab is not judged by a new rule but by the old one, and found in the worst company.

The witnesses draw from this the impartiality of the divine justice. God had raised up Baasha to destroy the house of Jeroboam and had then destroyed the house of Baasha in turn; now the house of Ahab is appointed to the same end. There is no royalty that exempts, no dynasty too established to fall, when idolatry and the shedding of innocent blood have provoked the LORD. The formula that once sounded against the founders of the kingdom now sounds against its most powerful king, and the reader is meant to hear in it the terrible evenhandedness of a God who visits the same sin with the same sentence, whatever the throne it sits upon.

The witnesses hear in the comparison the drumbeat of a settled divine principle. Jeroboam's house had been swept away, and Baasha — himself the instrument of that sweeping — had seen his own house destroyed in turn; now Ahab is enrolled in the same doomed line. Keil's citation of the ground ("thou hast provoked me to anger, and made Israel to sin") shows that Ahab is measured by the standing indictment of the northern kings, not by any new severity. What the reformed and grammatical-historical voices draw from this is the terrible evenhandedness of God. He is no respecter of dynasties; the sin that felled the founders of the kingdom will fell its mightiest king, and the same curse of dog and fowl already spoken over Jeroboam (1 Kings 14:11) and Baasha (1 Kings 16:4) is now spoken over Ahab. The verse offers no comfort of exception, only the sober warning that idolatry and innocent blood bring the same end upon whatever house commits them.
23:
The word against Jezebel is short and dreadful, and the witnesses gather around its literal fulfilment and its fitting shame. The reformed Gill notes only that "of Jezebel also spake the LORD" — the oracle spoken to and by Elijah — appointing that "the dogs shall eat Jezebel by the wall of Jezreel." The wesleyan-arminian Clarke insists the prophecy "was most literally fulfilled" (2 Kings 9:36), and illustrates the horror from the East, where "the carcasses of persons who have received public punishment are thrown into the rivers, and floating to the side, are devoured by dogs, vultures, and crows."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks the moment as the first time the prophet names the true instigator. "In all his address to Ahab, Elijah has, as yet, disdained to name" her, on whom "the coward king, no doubt, threw his guilt"; now, "briefly and sternly, the prophet notices the bolder criminal, pronouncing against her a doom of shame and horror, seldom falling upon a woman, but rightly visiting one who had forsworn the pity and modesty of her sex." Her body is to be flung "in the ditch outside the walls," where "the refuse of the city gathers the half-wild dogs," and there "torn and devoured as offal."

The witnesses do not divide. They read the sentence as exact retribution: the woman who had procured a murder "by the wall of Jezreel" will herself be eaten by dogs at that same wall; the queen who used the forms of law to shed innocent blood will receive the burial of a criminal, denied even the dignity of the grave. The bolder the crime, the plainer the judgment — and Elijah, having laid the guilt where it truly rested, on Ahab, now lays it also where it was hatched.

The witnesses read the sentence as retribution measured to the crime. It was "by the wall of Jezreel" that Jezebel's letters procured a murder; it is by that same wall that the dogs will devour her. The queen who used the solemn forms of law to deny a righteous man both life and honor is herself appointed the burial of the accursed — flung, in Ellicott's picture, into the ditch outside the walls "as offal," torn by the scavengers of the city. Clarke's insistence on the literal fulfilment (2 Kings 9:36) guards the verse from being softened into metaphor, while Gill's brevity lets the horror stand plain. And all three feel the significance of Elijah's timing: only now, having fixed the guilt on Ahab where the king had tried to shed it, does the prophet turn to name "the bolder criminal." The order matters. The man who profited by the murder is judged first, that no one may think the woman's greater guilt lessens his; and then the woman, that no one may think his lesser guilt clears her.
24:
The closing clause of the oracle rounds off the sentence with the sign of a curse, and though only the reformed Gill comments directly, his brief gloss unlocks the verse. "Him that dieth of Ahab in the city the dogs shall eat, and him that dieth in the field the fowls of the air shall eat" — that is, Gill says, "they shall have no burial." He adds the structural note that here Elijah's oracle ends: "hitherto are the words of the LORD by Elijah; next follow the remarks of the historian."

The verse's own imagery carries a weight the Israelite reader felt at once. To be left unburied, given to the scavenging dog in the town and the carrion bird in the open country, was the last extremity of shame, a curse pronounced elsewhere upon the very houses named in the preceding verses — Jeroboam's (1 Kings 14:11) and Baasha's (1 Kings 16:4). The completeness is deliberate: city and field, dog and bird, leaving nothing of Ahab's line honoured in death. The man who shed blood and confiscated a field to enlarge his house is told that his house shall have neither name nor grave.

The historian's frame, which Gill marks, matters for reading what follows. With this verse the prophetic word is finished; verses that come after are the compiler's own reflection on the king thus sentenced. The oracle has run its course from the accusation over the vineyard to the last indignity of the unburied dead, and the reader is invited to weigh, in the historian's coming verdict, just what manner of king had drawn down so total a doom — and then to watch, with some astonishment, how mercy will yet find a foothold even here.

The witnesses hear in the doubled image — the dog in the city, the bird in the field — the language of an ancient curse of utter extirpation. To lie unburied was, in Israel, the last dishonor, and here it is pronounced upon a whole dynasty, city and country alike, so that nothing of Ahab's house should be left to rest in a grave. The completeness is deliberate and answers the completeness of the crime: the king who confiscated a field and shed a family's blood to enlarge his own house is told his house shall have neither name nor tomb. Gill's structural note — that with this verse "the words of the LORD by Elijah" end and "the remarks of the historian" begin — invites the reader to pause over the sentence before the narrator renders his verdict. The oracle has run its dreadful course from the vineyard to the unburied dead; and it is precisely this total doom, spoken over a king "than whom none was worse," that makes the mercy of the chapter's last verses so astonishing.
25:
Here the narrative pauses for the historian's own verdict, and the witnesses read it as the fair summary of all that has gone before. The reformed Gill takes "there was none like unto Ahab" of his very predecessors, "even those whose families had been destroyed" — he outdid in wickedness the kings whose doom he was now to share. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke gives the phrase "did sell himself to work wickedness" its sharpest edge: "He hired himself to the devil for this very purpose, that he might work wickedness." And of the clause "whom Jezebel his wife stirred up," Clarke coins his memorable line: "A good wife is from the Lord; a bad wife is from the devil: Jezebel was of this kind; and she has had many successors."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott identifies the verse as "the reflection of the compiler, catching its inspiration from the words of Elijah" (v.20). Its tone, he says, is "not only of condemnation, but of contempt, for a king most unkingly — thus selling himself to a half-unwilling course of crime, against the warnings of conscience, not disbelieved but neglected, for the sake of a paltry desire," and "grovelling under the open dominion of a woman," which "to an Eastern mind would seem especially monstrous."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry completes the portrait. Ahab "made a trade" of sin, "sold himself to work wickedness," becoming "a perfect slave to his lusts," so that he took "the wages of it, which is death" (Rom. 6:23). Where blessed Paul complained of being "sold under sin" as a captive against his will (Rom. 7:14), "Ahab was voluntary: he sold himself." The witnesses agree without a dissenting voice: Jezebel's incitement excuses nothing, for the guilt was chosen; a man who lets a wicked counsel do what his own desire already craves has not been overpowered but has consented.

The witnesses agree that this compiler's verdict withholds every excuse Ahab might plead. Clarke's blunt "he hired himself to the devil" and Henry's "he made himself a perfect slave to his lusts" both fasten on the word sold: the wickedness was willed, not merely suffered. Where Paul groaned at being "sold under sin" against his will (Rom. 7:14), Ahab "was voluntary; he sold himself." And the clause "whom Jezebel his wife stirred up" is, in every reading, an aggravation rather than a mitigation — Ellicott's "grovelling under the open dominion of a woman," Clarke's grim epigram on the bad wife "from the devil." The commentators thus close the historian's portrait exactly where the chapter's action left it: a king who kept his conscience just clear enough to be guilty, who neglected warnings he did not disbelieve, and who took the wages of sin, "which is death" (Rom. 6:23), for the sake of a paltry desire. It is the fairest and the harshest thing said of Ahab, and it prepares the ground for the surprise of his sackcloth.
26:
The second stroke of the historian's verdict names the sin the witnesses reckon deepest of all — not the murder only, but the idolatry beneath it. The reformed Gill glosses "he did very abominably in following idols" as the worship of "dunghill gods," a doing "according to all things as did the Amorites, whom the LORD cast out before the children of Israel." The grammatical-historical Ellicott extends the charge past mere false worship: "the reference is probably not only to the idolatry and worship of false gods, but to the nameless abominations always connected with such worship."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch then mark the turn from indictment to remorse. "This terrible threat made such an impression upon Ahab, that he felt deep remorse, and for a time at least was sincerely penitent" — rending his clothes, putting on sackcloth, fasting, and walking about "lightly, slowly, like one in deep trouble." Keil states plainly the reading that the next verses will contest: "this repentance was neither hypocritical, nor purely external; but it was sincere, even if it was not lasting and produced no real conversion." His proof is that "the Lord Himself acknowledged it to be humiliation before Him" (v.29).

The witnesses thus place the verse at the hinge of the chapter. The historian's summary has piled the murder upon the idolatry and the idolatry upon the "abominations," painting a king with "none like" him in wickedness; and it is precisely this man, at the bottom of the indictment, whom the terrible word of Elijah bows into sackcloth. The greatness of the sin only sets in relief the greatness of the mercy about to appear, and the debate the commentators will now join — whether that mercy meets a true penitent or a frightened one — is already opening.

The witnesses register the force of setting Ahab's idolatry last in the indictment, as the root beneath the murder. Henry, surveying the whole, judges that "in many things he did ill, but he did most abominably in following idols," his immoralities provoking to God but his idolatries especially so. Ellicott's "nameless abominations" and Gill's "dunghill gods" both press the same point: the worship of the Amorites was not a private error but a corruption that hollowed out the conscience of a nation and made a Naboth's murder possible. Yet it is at this lowest point of the portrait that Keil marks the turn, insisting the coming repentance was "sincere, even if it was not lasting," and vindicated by God's own acknowledgment of it (v.29). The witnesses thus feel the chapter tightening toward its paradox. The blacker the historian paints the king, the more startling the sackcloth; and the very depth of the abomination named here is what will make the mercy of the closing verses a wonder rather than a reward.
27:
Ahab's sudden humiliation opens the chapter's sharpest debate, and the witnesses divide over the very thing the verse describes. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke takes the signs at full value: rending his clothes and lying in sackcloth, Ahab "was penetrated with sorrow, and that evidently unfeigned." He gathers the versions on the phrase "went softly" — barefooted, by the Chaldee, Syriac, and Arabic; "with head hanging down," by the Vulgate; "went groaning," by Houbigant; "unshod," by Jarchi — and concludes that "all these things prove that Ahab's repentance was genuine; and God's approbation of it puts it out of doubt," comparing the "slow and measured pace" of grief in Aeschylus. The grammatical-historical Ellicott agrees the translation is "correct," judging "barefooted" of the disputed phrase "far the most probable meaning," and Keil and Delitzsch, as noted, call the sorrow "sincere even if not lasting."

Against this stands the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, who sees a real fright but not a changed heart. Ahab was "still a proud hardened sinner, and yet thus reduced"; he "put on the garb and guise of a penitent, and yet his heart was unhumbled and unchanged." Henry's evidence is the sequel — soon Ahab "hated a faithful prophet" — and his verdict is that the repentance "was external only, the garments rent, but not the heart," for "a hypocrite may go very far in the outward performance of holy duties and yet come short."

Yet even Henry grants what all the witnesses confess: that "God can make the stoutest heart to tremble," for His word "is quick and powerful," "like a fire and a hammer" (Jer. 23:29), the same word that made Felix tremble. The division, then, is over the depth of Ahab's sorrow, not the power that produced it; and it is a division the next verse will not so much settle as transcend, when God Himself passes judgment on the humbling.

The witnesses locate the whole difficulty in the gap between the outward signs and the inward heart. Clarke, reading the signs, finds the sorrow "unfeigned" and its acceptance by God decisive: "God's approbation of it puts it out of doubt." Henry, reading the sequel, finds "the garments rent, but not the heart," a penitence "external only" that soon gave way to fresh hatred of a faithful prophet. Keil and Ellicott stand nearer Clarke, calling the humiliation real if not lasting. What none of them denies is the power that produced it. Even Henry, most skeptical of Ahab's heart, marvels that "God can make the stoutest heart to tremble, and the proudest to humble itself," his word being "quick and powerful," "like a fire and a hammer" (Jer. 23:29). So the debate is not whether the word of doom struck home — it plainly did, bowing a proud king into sackcloth — but whether the sorrow it wrung out was godly grief or only the fear of ruin; and that question the LORD Himself will weigh in the verse to come.
28:
The word of the LORD comes to Elijah a second time, and the witnesses mark the turn from the language of doom to the language of clemency. The reformed Gill notes that the message came "after Ahab had been some time in this humble posture," and that the Targum names it, as it had named the first oracle, "the word of prophecy" — for it too foretells, though now it foretells a deferral. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the substance: "Seest thou how Ahab humbleth himself before me? because he humbleth himself before me, I will not bring the evil in his days: but in his son's days will I bring the evil upon his house."

The witnesses feel the wonder of the moment before they expound it. The same prophet who was sent down to the vineyard to pronounce a total ruin — the dogs, the fowls, the extirpated house — is now sent to announce that the ruin will be postponed. The instrument of judgment becomes the instrument of mercy, and the God who "is of purer eyes than to behold iniquity" shows Himself also the God who "will not bring the evil in his days" the moment a wicked man bends before Him.

The verse turns, moreover, on a divine question — "Seest thou?" — addressed to the very prophet who had every reason to expect the sentence carried out in full. It is as if the LORD would have His servant, and through him the reader, take note of a thing worth marvelling at: that a humiliation so partial, offered by a man so guilty, should be weighed at all. The sentence itself is not revoked but adjourned; the debt stands, and will be paid in the son's day. But that any reprieve should follow such a life is the astonishment the next verse will name outright, and that the commentators will divide over even as they adore.

The witnesses dwell on the mercy that the second oracle reveals. The very prophet dispatched to the vineyard with a sentence of total ruin is now dispatched with a word of reprieve; the messenger of doom becomes the messenger of clemency. Gill's note that this too is "the word of prophecy" reminds us that the deferral is as surely God's decree as the destruction was: the evil is not cancelled but adjourned, to fall "in his son's days." And the form of the word — a question put to Elijah, "Seest thou how Ahab humbleth himself?" — invites the prophet, who had every reason to expect the sentence executed in full, to take note of a thing worth marvelling at. The commentators feel the astonishment before they analyze it. That a humiliation so partial, offered by a king so guilty, should be weighed at all is the wonder; and it discloses a God who, "of purer eyes than to behold iniquity," is yet swift to relent at the first bending of a hard heart, and who will not bring the threatened evil "in his days."
29:
On the chapter's closing verse the witnesses are at once divided over Ahab and united in wonder at God. The reformed Gill judges the humiliation "external only, to be seen with bodily eyes": Ahab "never reproved Jezebel for the murder of Naboth, nor restored the vineyard to his family," nor "did he leave his idols," and his sorrow "arose from dread of punishment, and not from the true fear of God" — yet "it was such as was never seen in any of his wicked predecessors, and is taken notice of by the Lord." The wesleyan-arminian Clarke reads it far more warmly: "He did truly repent... and it was such a repentance as was genuine in the sight of God," for "the penitent heart ever meets the merciful eye of God," and "any measure of godly sorrow has a proportionate measure of God's regard."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott holds the balance. The repentance "might seem not only to come too late, but to be the mere offspring of fear — more sensible of the shame of discovery than of the shamefulness of sin"; "man's judgment would despise it," yet "God sees in its imperfection some germs of promise," and "His partial remission of penalty shows it to be not disregarded in His sight." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns it to encouragement: God's goodness here is "rejoicing against judgment," teaching us "to take notice of that which is good even in those who are not so good as they should be" — and to trust that "if a pretending partial penitent shall go to his house reprieved, doubtless a sincere penitent shall go to his house justified."

Gill records, as an outlier, that Luther, pondering these words, "was persuaded" to a more hopeful verdict on Ahab than the plain narrative will bear. But the witnesses land together where the text lands: on the largeness of a mercy that stoops even to a half-hearted penitent. Whatever Ahab's heart, the God who spared not the houses of Jeroboam and Baasha (1 Kings 16:4) will "not bring the evil in his days" — proof, the commentators agree, that heaven prizes even the faintest turning, and that no measure of true sorrow is ever wholly despised.

The witnesses close the chapter where its whole weight has been tending: on the disproportion between Ahab's slight turning and God's large regard for it. Whether they judge the repentance false (Gill, Henry) or genuine in its measure (Clarke, Keil, Ellicott), they agree that the reprieve is an act of sheer mercy, "goodness rejoicing against judgment," and that it teaches us "to take notice of that which is good even in those who are not so good as they should be."
